conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let me start by reading the official guidance to ensure I provide you with accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit. 

Let me start with the first question:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and would like to know if I'm eligible. I have one child - my son Alex, who's 17.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have one child, Alex, who is 17 years old.

Since Alex is 17, he's over the age of 16, which means there are specific rules that apply. To determine your eligibility, I need to ask:

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, he's not in education or training at the moment. He left school recently, but he has signed up with the local careers service to figure out what he wants to do next.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's helpful to know that Alex has registered with the local careers service after leaving education.

To make sure I have all the details I need:

**When did Alex leave education or training - do you know approximately how many weeks ago that was?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been about 10 weeks or so since he left school.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. Let me ask one more question to complete my assessment:

**Do you live in the UK, and does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Manchester actually - and Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the official UK government guidance, let me explain the outcome:

**You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why: According to the rules, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services.

In your case:
- Alex is 17 years old
- He left school approximately 10 weeks ago
- He has registered with the local careers service
- He lives with you in the UK

Since Alex registered with the careers service and it's only been 10 weeks since he left education (which is within the 20-week period), you remain eligible for Child Benefit for him during this time.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Please note that this eligibility will last for up to 20 weeks from when Alex left education, so you have approximately another 10 weeks of eligibility remaining under this provision. After that, you would only continue to be eligible if Alex enters approved education or training.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

circumstances: Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and left education approximately 10 weeks ago. According to UK Child Benefit rules, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Alex has registered with the local careers service, and only 10 weeks have passed since he left school, which is within the 20-week eligibility period. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) and Alex lives with them, satisfying the residency and responsibility requirements. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 17-year-old son Alex. Although Alex has left education, he registered with the local careers service, which allows Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks. Since only 10 weeks have passed, the claimant remains within the eligible period and meets all other requirements (UK residency and living with the child).
